abstract:
Calculating nutrient density based on standard metrics—such as 100 kcal, 100 g/mL, 50 g, or the Reference Amount Customarily Consumed (RACC)—often produces a distorted representation of a food's true nutritional value. Reliance on these metrics for dietary selection can inadvertently increase the risk of chronic disease by promoting foods with poor nutritional profiles. This study evaluated 8,596 food items across 25 categories and 29 food components by comparing the regulatory standards of the Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC) and the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) against a proposed optimized procedure.

Our findings indicate that selecting foods based on current FDA and CAC guidelines frequently results in energy intake exceeding metabolic requirements when attempting to meet targets for beneficial nutrients (such as vitamins, protein, dietary fiber, and minerals). Conversely, many foods categorized as acceptable under CAC guidelines failed to meet essential nutrient demands. Furthermore, food selections based on these standard metrics often led to the overconsumption of "negative" components, including cholesterol, total fat, saturated fat, and sodium. Many nutritionally sound options were paradoxically excluded by existing regulatory frameworks. 

The analysis reveals significant vulnerabilities in current food selection models. When evaluated using the refined scoring system for nutritional quality, the highest-rated categories across all age groups (children over four and adults) were fast foods (evaluated by serving size under CAC/FDA), spices and herbs (per 100 g/mL under CAC), and vegetables (per 100 kcal under CAC). These results underscore the need for more nuanced reference standards to better align dietary choices with actual health outcomes.
